Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar Sep-10-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom Conference
LEE E. SHELDON, Esq., LITTLER MENDELSON 415.433.1940 lsheldon@littler.com Attorney for Defendant, GOLDEN GATE DISPOSAL & RECYCLING COMPANY
No other appearances

Cousel appears out of an abundance of caution.

No argument is held. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT GOLDEN GATE DISPOSAL & RECYCLING COMPANY Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Stay Proceedings

Defendant Goden Gate Disposal & Recycling Co.'s (Golden Gate) motion to compel arbitration is granted. The proceedings are stayed pending the conclusion of the arbitration.

Plaintiff Raul Alvarez (Alvarez) alleges that Golden Gate discriminated against him, harassed him, retaliated against him and eventually terminated him due to his cumulative injuries and requests for reasonable accommodations. (Complaint, 12-20.) Alvarez brought claims for discrimination, harassment, retaliation, failure to prevent the aforementioned, failure to provide reasonable accommodations, wrongful termination and breach of implied contract. Golden Gate now brings this motion to compel arbitration and dismiss proceedings.

It is the burden of the party seeking to compel arbitration to establish the existence of an enforceable arbitration agreement. (Diaz v. Sohnen Enterprises (2019) 34 Cal.App.5th 126, 129.) "A movant can bear this initial burden 'by attaching a copy of the arbitration agreement purportedly bearing the opposing party's signature.'" (Iyere v. Wise Auto Group (2023) 87 Cal.App.5th 747, 755, citing Espejo v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1047, 1060.) During this first step, a moving party need "not follow the normal procedures of document authentication" and need only "allege the existence of an agreement and support the allegation as provided in [California Rule of Court] Rule [3.1330]." (Id.) Golden Gate meets its burden by attaching a copy of the signed arbitration agreement. (Declaration of Kristina Cox, Exhibit A.)

The burden shifts to Alvarez to either dispute the agreement's existence or provide a defense to enforcement, in this case, unconscionability. The agreement is not substantively unconscionable. The agreement's carve-out allowing Golden Gate to pursue injunctive relief in court to the extent it is entitled to it is a standard provision that protects Golden Gate's legitimate commercial need to protect its proprietary and confidential information from public disclosure. (Baltazar v. Forever 21, Inc. (2016) 62 Cal.4th 1237, 1250.) The American Arbitration Association (AAA) rules provide that Alvarez would only be responsible for Golden Gate's attorney's fees in accordance with applicable law. (Declaration of Lee Sheldon, Exhibit D, Rule 39(d).) Golden Gate has further acknowledged that it will cover all costs unique to the arbitration. (Id., Exhibit A.) The AAA rules do not impermissibly limit remedies or discovery, and a written award is provided for. (Id., Exhibit D.) Because both procedural and substantive unconscionability must be shown for the defense to be established, the agreement is not unconscionable. (OTO, L.L.C. v. Kho (2019) 8 Cal.5th 111, 125 (citing Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 114).)

The motion is granted and the case is stayed pending the conclusion of the arbitration under CCP 1281.4.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)
